strategy_research: {"strategies": [{"name": "Antonacci's Global Equities Momentum (GEM / Dual Momentum)", "description": "Monthly rotation using two momentum layers: absolute momentum compares SPY vs. T-bills to determine whether equities deserve capital at all; relative momentum then picks SPY vs. EFA (U.S. vs. international). If SPY fails the absolute test, entire portfolio shifts to AGG as safe haven. One trade per month at most.", "evidence": ["Antonacci (2012/2014) 'Risk Premia Harvesting Through Dual Momentum' \u2014 foundational paper with factor-model regression showing statistically significant alpha across Fama-French 3, 4, and 5-factor models", "Book: 'Dual Momentum Investing' (McGraw-Hill, 2014)", "Out-of-sample windows: 1950\u20131973 (pre-formation) and 2012\u20132018 (post-publication) both show consistent performance", "InvestResolve replication and Allocate Smartly tracking confirm live performance, though with decay post-2012"], "historical_sharpe": "0.87 (1974\u20132013 backtest); max drawdown -22.7% vs. -60% benchmark; CAGR ~17% in-sample (optimistic). Post-2012 live performance has lagged SPY significantly.", "market_conditions": "Works: bear markets with clean trend breaks (2000\u201302, 2008\u201309), trending environments with clear U.S./international leadership divergence. Fails: prolonged U.S. equity dominance (2012\u20132024), simultaneous equity+bond drawdowns (2022), choppy/mean-reverting markets triggering false switches, rising-rate regimes eroding bond safe-haven value.", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Keller & Keuning VAA (Vigilant Asset Allocation)", "description": "Breadth-based momentum rotation: rank 4 offensive assets (SPY, EFA, EEM, AGG) by weighted momentum (12:4:2:1 ratio of 1/3/6/12-month returns). If ANY offensive asset has negative momentum, rotate 100% to the best-performing defensive asset (SHY, IEF, or LQD). Binary, concentrated positioning \u2014 always 100% in one ETF.", "evidence": ["Keller & Keuning (2017) 'Breadth Momentum and Vigilant Asset Allocation (VAA): Winning More by Losing Less' \u2014 SSRN abstract_id=3002624", "Backtest data from 1925 (U.S.) and 1969 (global ETF-equivalent), four distinct asset universes tested", "Allocate Smartly live tracking since 2017 confirms crash-protection properties", "Keller Ratio (CAGR \u00d7 drawdown-adjusted factor) shown superior to Sharpe for this strategy class"], "historical_sharpe": "~1.10 (VAA-G4 backtest); max drawdown -16.1%; CAGR ~10\u201312% net. Sharpe falls to 0.83 if AGG is removed from offensive universe, raising overfitting concerns.", "market_conditions": "Works: sharp crashes where breadth deteriorates early (2008, March 2020), trending bull markets with identifiable leadership. Fails: simultaneous stock+bond declines (2022), choppy markets causing whipsaw from recency-weighted momentum, narrow bull markets where strategy sits in cash and misses the rally.", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "IWM", "AGG", "TLT", "GLD"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Cross-Asset Momentum Ranking with Trend Filter and Volatility Targeting", "description": "Three-layer systematic approach: (1) Rank available ETFs by risk-adjusted momentum (12-1 month return / realized vol); (2) apply trend filter \u2014 only hold assets above 200-day MA; (3) size positions so each contributes equal realized volatility (inverse-vol weighting), scaled to a portfolio-level vol target (e.g., 10% annualized). Rebalance monthly or weekly.", "evidence": ["Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012) 'Time Series Momentum' \u2014 Journal of Financial Economics; 58 futures markets, diversified Sharpe ~1.0+ out-of-sample", "Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen (2012/2014) \u2014 extended time-series momentum evidence back to 1903", "J.P. Morgan Quantitative Strategy (2015, CME Group) \u2014 EMV-weighted cross-asset momentum Sharpe 0.78 across equities/FX/commodities/bonds (1972\u20132014)", "Harvey et al. / Man Group (2018) 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting' \u2014 vol targeting improves Sharpe most for equity and credit momentum legs", "EFMA (2013) 'Risk Parity, Momentum and Trend Following in Global Asset Allocation' \u2014 combining ranking + trend filter raises Sharpe 0.3\u20130.5 above momentum-only"], "historical_sharpe": "0.95\u20131.25 for combined strategy (momentum + trend + vol target); individual components range 0.5\u20130.8 momentum alone, +0.3\u20130.5 improvement from trend filter, further improvement from vol targeting.", "market_conditions": "Works: trending markets in any direction, high inter-asset dispersion, crisis periods (positive skew via trend filter). Fails: rapid trend reversals (March 2020 recovery), low-vol consolidation phases, high cross-asset correlation removing diversification, post-crisis V-shaped recoveries where vol targeting forces size reduction before rebound.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG", "VTI"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Regime-Based Rotation Using VIX Term Structure and Yield Curve", "description": "Two orthogonal regime signals applied as a portfolio overlay: (1) VIX level + term structure \u2014 VIX < 18 and in contango = risk-on, hold equities; VIX > 25 or in backwardation = risk-off, rotate to short-duration bonds; (2) Yield curve \u2014 sustained 2Y > 10Y inversion shifts allocation toward short-duration bonds and defensives. Require both signals to agree before major reallocation to reduce false positives.", "evidence": ["Chicago Fed (2018) \u2014 yield curve (10Y-3M) correctly preceded 7 of 8 recessions since 1968 with 7\u201324 month lead", "PLOS ONE (2024) 'VIX Constant Maturity Futures Trading Strategy: A Walk-Forward Backtest' \u2014 XGBoost with term structure features, information ratio 0.632 and 11.6% annualized return", "ECB Working Paper No. 472 (2005) \u2014 regime-switching yield curve factor model with macro-linked transition probabilities", "Dimensional Fund Advisors research \u2014 important counterpoint: yield curve inversion does NOT reliably predict near-term equity returns (confirms it as a risk signal, not a market-timing trigger)"], "historical_sharpe": "0.85\u20131.05 as overlay; variable depending on signal thresholds. Best implemented as a 20\u201340% equity weight reducer when both signals are bearish rather than binary all-in/all-out switching.", "market_conditions": "Works: gradual bear markets preceded by macro deterioration, recession environments accompanied by Fed easing (bonds as effective refuge), normal inflation regimes. Fails: V-shaped recoveries (whipsaw), stagflation (both signals ambiguous), QE/QT distortion periods masking yield curve signals, high-speed shocks like March 2020.", "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP) \u2014 Lopez de Prado", "description": "Graph-theoretic portfolio construction that avoids inverting the covariance matrix (the primary failure mode of Markowitz). Steps: (1) cluster assets by correlation distance into a hierarchical tree; (2) quasi-diagonalize the covariance matrix; (3) recursively bisect the tree and allocate risk top-down, weighting each cluster inversely proportional to its variance. Best used as the allocation engine inside a momentum or rotation framework rather than standalone.", "evidence": ["L\u00f3pez de Prado (2016) 'Building Diversified Portfolios that Outperform Out of Sample' \u2014 Journal of Portfolio Management 42(4), pp. 59\u201369; SSRN abstract_id=2708678", "Monte Carlo experiments show HRP achieves ~31.3% better out-of-sample Sharpe vs. Markowitz CLA (critical-line algorithm)", "HRP produces lowest out-of-sample variance among tested methods: Minimum Variance, Inverse Variance, Equal Weight, and HRP", "Independent academic replications on real equity data confirm outperformance of equal-weight and risk-parity baselines in out-of-sample Sharpe"], "historical_sharpe": "+31% Sharpe improvement vs. Markowitz CLA in out-of-sample Monte Carlo; absolute Sharpe depends on asset universe. Note: raw HRP turnover is ~3x higher than equal-risk contribution variants \u2014 net of transaction costs the advantage narrows.", "market_conditions": "Works: highly correlated asset universes with stable cluster structure, larger asset universes (20+ assets), ill-conditioned covariance matrices. Fails: very small asset universes where inverse-vol weighting is more robust, unstable correlation regimes that restructure the cluster tree mid-period.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "VTI", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG", "EFA"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Stacked Momentum Ensemble (Multi-Signal Rotation)", "description": "Combine 3\u20135 independently validated, low-correlation momentum/trend signals and average their position signals before execution. Example stack for a retail TAA investor: (1) 12-month absolute momentum (Antonacci); (2) 200-day trend filter; (3) 1-month reversal dampener (avoid chasing last month's winner); (4) yield curve regime overlay; (5) cross-asset lead-lag (e.g., TLT vs. SPY recent divergence). Equal-weight each signal's binary output, then size positions proportionally.", "evidence": ["Hafez, Navarro, Gomez, Kangrga (SSRN 2023) 'Three Trading Signals from Earnings Conference Calls' \u2014 combined IR 1.4 (large-cap) and 2.3 (small-cap) vs. individual signal IR ~0.6\u20130.9", "arXiv (2025) 'Revisiting Ensemble Methods for Stock and Crypto Trading' \u2014 ensemble models cut return variance roughly by half vs. individual agents", "Signal combination theory: for N uncorrelated signals each with Sharpe S, combined Sharpe \u2248 S \u00d7 sqrt(N) \u2014 theoretical ceiling for decorrelated combination", "AQR Capital research on factor diversification (Asness et al.) demonstrates that combining value, momentum, carry, and defensive factors achieves Sharpe ~1.0+ out-of-sample with lower individual signal quality needed"], "historical_sharpe": "0.95\u20131.30 for well-constructed 3\u20135 signal ensembles; information ratio 1.4\u20132.3 in best-documented academic examples. Retail-implementable version (3\u20134 macro momentum signals) more likely to achieve Sharpe 0.9\u20131.1.", "market_conditions": "Works: environments where multiple orthogonal return drivers are simultaneously active, diverse macro regimes. Fails: when signals become correlated (crisis conditions synchronize previously independent factors), when individual signal alpha has decayed, when excessive turnover from multiple signals exceeds edge.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "high"}, {"name": "Intermarket Divergence Filter (Gold\u2013Bond Disagreement)", "description": "Exploit the breakdown of the normally positive gold\u2013bond correlation to detect macro regime transitions. Normal: both GLD and TLT rise together (flight-to-quality). Divergence signal: GLD up + TLT down = inflation/reflation regime (favor equities, commodities, short bonds); GLD down + TLT up = deflationary recession signal (favor long bonds, reduce equities). Applied as a confirmation filter or position-sizing overlay, not a standalone system.", "evidence": ["Murphy (2004) 'Intermarket Analysis: Profiting from Global Market Relationships' (Wiley) \u2014 foundational text establishing gold-bonds-dollar-equities framework", "EasyLanguage/TradeStation practitioner community \u2014 intermarket divergence models on 30-year Treasuries shown robust 17+ years post-development without re-optimization", "Academic corroboration: flight-to-quality assets (gold and bonds) historically show positive correlation during risk-off, breakdown predicts regime change (extensive macro finance literature)", "Bollinger (1998) formalized the divergence concept for institutional use; widely applied in rates and FX desks"], "historical_sharpe": "Not formalized as standalone \u2014 best documented as a filter improving Sharpe of base strategies by 0.1\u20130.2. Practitioner evidence suggests robust signals over 17+ year windows without re-optimization.", "market_conditions": "Works: macro regime transitions (inflation vs. deflation pivots), pre-recession positioning, periods when correlations reassert themselves after temporary divergence. Fails: QE/QT periods where Fed intervention decouples natural relationships, currency crisis environments, short-term noise (requires weekly/monthly timeframe).", "instruments": ["GLD", "TLT", "SPY", "AGG"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Tax-Loss Harvesting with January Rebound Capture", "description": "Two-phase calendar strategy: (1) December harvest \u2014 sell YTD losers before Dec 31 to realize tax losses, immediately deploy to a non-substantially-identical substitute (e.g., sell SPY, buy VTI) to stay invested; (2) January rebound \u2014 screen for small-cap ETFs or individual stocks that were YTD losers in prior year, buy in late December or first five trading days of January to capture the tax-selling-reversal bounce. Effect is strongest for small-caps (IWM) and less liquid assets.", "evidence": ["Wachtel (1942) Journal of Business \u2014 first documented January Effect: average January return 3.5% vs. 0.5% other months (1925\u20131942)", "Reinganum (1983) and Roll (1983) \u2014 linked January effect mechanistically to December tax-loss selling pressure on small-cap stocks", "Zheng, Li et al. (University of Michigan) \u2014 muni bond CEFs: average January return 2.21% vs. -0.19% for other 11 months", "ScienceDirect (2022) 'Tax-Loss Harvesting Under Uncertainty' \u2014 December losses most valuable due to tax rate uncertainty resolving at year-end; quantifies the calendar timing premium", "Canaccord Genuity \u2014 stocks down >15% YTD underperform ~4% mid-Nov to mid-Dec, then outperform ~3.6% mid-Dec to mid-Jan"], "historical_sharpe": "Not applicable as standalone Sharpe metric \u2014 effect generates ~2\u20133.5% January premium for small caps historically, declining from ~4.2% pre-1990s to ~2.9% post-2000 due to increased tax-advantaged account penetration. Tax alpha from harvesting is guaranteed regardless of market conditions.", "market_conditions": "Works: years with large YTD loser cohort (bear markets, sector crashes), low tax-advantaged account penetration environments, high institutional window-dressing activity. Fails: strong bull markets with few losers to harvest, years with early institutional pre-positioning reducing January bounce, modern high-401k/IRA environment.", "instruments": ["IWM", "SPY", "VTI", "QQQ"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}], "recommendation": "Start with the Cross-Asset Momentum Ranking with Trend Filter and Volatility Targeting strategy, implemented in a simplified retail-friendly form: rank all 8 ETFs (SPY, QQQ, IWM, VTI, GLD, TLT, AGG, EFA) by 12-1 month return divided by 12-month realized volatility; only hold the top 3 ranked assets that are also above their 200-day MA; size each position by inverse realized volatility (equal vol contribution). Rebalance monthly. This approach has the best combination of academic support (Moskowitz 2012, Hurst 2014, JPM 2015), implementable complexity at $1000 scale (3 ETFs, monthly trades), and genuine out-of-sample evidence across multiple asset classes and decades. Its multi-asset scope means it benefits from true diversification across equities, bonds, and gold \u2014 avoiding the bond-as-safe-haven failure of GEM and VAA in 2022. Supplement with the Intermarket Divergence Filter as a confirmation check: if GLD and TLT are diverging, increase confidence in the momentum signal's regime read. Layer in Tax-Loss Harvesting each December at zero incremental cost for guaranteed tax alpha. Avoid VAA as a first implementation \u2014 its 100% concentration and recency-weighted momentum caused severe whipsaw in 2022\u20132023, and its published Sharpe is sensitive to whether AGG is included as an offensive asset (a questionable design choice suggesting some in-sample optimization)."}
